source_excerpt: "Yes," answered the tin man, "I did. I've been groaning for more than a year, and no one has ever heard me before or come to help me."

dialogue_text: What can I do for you?
source_excerpt: "What can I do for you?" she inquired softly, for she was moved by the sad voice in which the man spoke.

speaker.notes: Attributed through a role label rather than a canonical name.

dialogue_text: Get an oil-can and oil my joints,
source_excerpt: "Get an oil-can and oil my joints," he answered. "They are rusted so badly that I cannot move them at all; if I am well oiled I shall soon be all right again. You will find an oil-can on a shelf in my cottage."

speaker.notes: No canonical speaker could be inferred from the source context.

source_excerpt: Dorothy at once ran back to the cottage and found the oil-can, and then she returned and asked anxiously, "Where are your joints?"

speaker.notes: No canonical speaker could be inferred from the source context.

review_notes: Speaker could not be resolved from the source context.

source_excerpt: "Oil my neck, first," replied the Tin Woodman. So she oiled it, and as it was quite badly rusted the Scarecrow took hold of the tin head and moved it gently from side to side until it worked freely, and then the man could turn it himself.

dialogue_text: Now oil the joints in my arms,
source_excerpt: "Now oil the joints in my arms," he said. And Dorothy oiled them and the Scarecrow bent them carefully until they were quite free from rust and as good as new.

speaker.notes: No canonical speaker could be inferred from the source context.

review_notes: Speaker could not be resolved from the source context.

source_excerpt: "This is a great comfort," he said. "I have been holding that axe in the air ever since I rusted, and I'm glad to be able to put it down at last. Now, if you will oil the joints of my legs, I shall be all right once more."
